Research journey · in progress

Indistinguishability using differential privacy methods for protecting timing behavior

1 Why this matters Click headings to reveal
Problem

The problem: timing behavior is observable.

I have scheduler-side information about runtime behavior, and with this information I can carry out timing attacks: recover execution intervals, analyze them against a victim task, and infer or predict future victim arrivals.

These attacks are dangerous because the schedule itself becomes a side channel. Even when the application data is protected, repeated timing patterns can reveal enough structure for control-signal overriding, cache-timing side channels, and other attacks against real-time autonomous systems.
Goal

The goal: make runtime behavior indistinguishable.

What I want to do is this: use differential privacy as indistinguishability. I am applying noise to runtime behavior in a systematic manner so that nearby schedules produce outputs that are hard for an attacker to tell apart.

The defense intentionally perturbs when runtime events are released or scheduled. The noise is calibrated by a privacy budget, so the mechanism is not random guessing; it is a controlled tradeoff between privacy and real-time utility.

Indistinguishability

$$\Pr[\mathcal{M}(r) \in S] \le e^{\varepsilon}\Pr[\mathcal{M}(r') \in S]$$

For neighboring runtime traces $r$ and $r'$, the released behavior should look almost the same.

Runtime-noise release

$$\widetilde{r}_i(t) = r_i(t) + Y, \qquad Y \sim \mathrm{Lap}\!\left(0,\frac{\Delta r}{\varepsilon}\right)$$

The noise scale grows with runtime sensitivity $\Delta r$ and shrinks as the privacy budget $\varepsilon$ increases.

Trajectory results

The system should still work correctly.

We want the system to keep following the correct trajectory in spite of adding noise. The path followed by the system with noise is very close to the real thing. But now, attackers cannot look at the timing information and infer the runtime behavior.

The goal is both utility and privacy: the controller still behaves correctly, while the attacker cannot reliably infer timing behavior from the observed schedule.

1

The problem: vulnerability of determinism
"Predictability is a double-edged sword."

Real-time systems need deterministic timing for safety, but that same timing can expose the execution pattern.

  • Predictability helps the system meet deadlines
  • Repeated timing helps attackers reconstruct behavior
  • The foundation question: how can we keep safety while hiding timing?
2

Differential privacy
"The same DP idea, renamed for schedules."
$$\Pr[\,\mathcal{M}(D) \in S\,] \;\le\; e^{\varepsilon}\;\Pr[\,\mathcal{M}(D') \in S\,]$$
3

Schedule indistinguishability
"Inject controlled Laplace noise into the scheduler."

The attacker should not be able to distinguish nearby schedules from timing observations.

  • Noise is added to task arrival / release behavior
  • Periodic patterns become harder to infer
  • $\varepsilon$ controls the privacy-utility tradeoff
4

Apply bounded Laplace to timing
$$\mathcal{R}(\tau_i, j) \;=\; \eta_i(j) + Y$$ $$Y \sim \mathrm{Lap}(0,\, b)$$ $$b \;=\; \frac{J \cdot \Delta\eta_\Gamma}{\varepsilon}$$ $$\mathcal{R} \in [\,T^{\bot},\, T^{\top}\,]$$ $$\Delta\eta_\Gamma \;=\; \max_{\tau,\tau'\in\Gamma,\; j,j'} |\eta_\tau(j) - \eta_{\tau'}(j')|$$
$\eta_i(j)$
— nominal inter-arrival time of task $\tau_i$ at its $j$-th job
$\mathcal{R}(\tau_i,j)$
— the released (noisy) inter-arrival time
$Y$
— Laplace random noise, zero mean, scale $b$
$b$
— the noise scale — bigger $b$ = noisier outputs
$\Delta\eta_\Gamma$
— sensitivity: the largest gap between any two task / job inter-arrival times
$J$
— number of jobs observed (composition factor, Thm 4.3)
$\varepsilon$
— privacy budget, brought in from Card 3
$[T^{\bot},T^{\top}]$
— admissible period range; clipping doubles variance to offset bias
1Problem
2DP
3Indist.
4Apply DP
5Result
6Limit
5

Result — rover & video streaming
"QoS holds; attacker's inference drops."
  • Top row — typical RTS: periodic, deterministic, predictable
  • Bottom row — $\varepsilon$-Scheduler: diversified arrivals, indistinguishable schedules
  • Demonstrated on autonomous rover and video streaming over the internet
6

Limitation / next step
  • One noise family is tried
  • Noise is clipped
  • We need to understand the noise models better
  • We need to think about how to integrate them
Preliminary work
From single-node scheduling noise to network timing leakage.
Problem
  • On one node, adding noise to a rover scheduler is useful.
  • In real critical systems, nodes also communicate.
  • That network timing can become the next side channel.
What I did

I built a client–server testbed where periodic task behavior crosses the network and two attackers try to reconstruct timing.

Why it matters

Even if individual nodes are protected, traffic timing can still reveal structure. Protecting the ratio between utility and privacy is harder in this setting.

A. The testbed & attack
Client
Raspberry Pi

Sends real periodic tasks over the wire.

A Attacker A · injector
  • Lives on the client side
  • Injects a fake task into the transmission line
transmission
line
Server
Mac · EDF

Schedules tasks, logs execution timing.

B Attacker B · observer
  • Reads server-side logs
  • Knows the fake task's parameters
  • Knows one real task's period
!
Together, A & B recover the victim task's period & offset — ScheduLeak-style.
B. Seven noise mechanisms tested
The noise graph is useful because the foundation paper clips the noise. That limitation becomes the launching point for this work: understand the noise models better, compare bounded and unbounded variants, and think about how these mechanisms should be integrated into a networked real-time system.
Current insight
3 slots still leaking.
Problem

There is still leakage of behavior. Even after adding timing noise, the attacker can recover structure from the schedule.

What I did

I tested the timing-noise layer across the noise mechanisms and measured leaked slots and missed deadlines.

Results across the 7 mechanisms
Leaked slots

lower is more private — attacker recovers fewer time slots

Missed deadlines

lower is better — system meets its real-time guarantees

What I want to do

Add a second layer inspired by TaskShuffler randomized scheduling, so task selection also becomes harder to infer.

Limitations of TaskShuffler
  • TaskShuffler wants to meet every single deadline, so the randomization is limited in scope.
  • The DP noise application is also limited: you cannot apply DP freely if every deadline must always be met.
  • Because it is not a formal DP mechanism, there is no clear way to measure how much security or privacy the system adds.
Why this points to layer 2
Timing noise alone is limited: privacy improves, but behavior can still leak and deadline cost appears.

The next step is to protect the scheduling decision itself. A TaskShuffler-style layer can randomize which eligible task runs, but its original goal is to meet every deadline, so its privacy scope is limited and not formally DP.

Step 1

TaskShuffler

Limitation: TaskShuffler wants to meet every single deadline, so the randomization is limited in scope.

Step 2

What I looked at

  • TaskShuffler randomizes schedules by choosing among eligible jobs
  • It uses an inversion budget $V_i$ so deadlines are still met
  • This is useful because it attacks the remaining deterministic scheduling pattern
  • But it is not DP: the random choice does not come with a privacy budget
Step 3

Exponential DP

  • Build a second layer inspired by TaskShuffler, but with a DP selection rule
  • Use feasibility / slack to avoid destroying real-time behavior
  • Use an exponential-mechanism style choice over eligible jobs
  • Goal: combine timing noise with randomized task selection while making the deadline/privacy tradeoff explicit

I want to tackle these 3 things

Question 1

What is the right privacy unit?

  • One task? one job? one inter-arrival time? one network event?
  • The answer changes the meaning of neighboring schedules and the privacy budget.
Question 2

How much deadline risk is acceptable?

  • Strictly meeting every deadline limits the amount of useful randomization.
  • We need to define the tradeoff between schedulability, utility, and privacy.
Question 3

How should the two layers compose?

  • Timing noise protects arrivals and run-time behavior.
  • Task-selection randomization protects the scheduling decision.
  • The open question is how to account for the joint privacy loss.

Output probability under one task is at most $e^\varepsilon$ times the probability under another task. Standard DP, applied to inter-arrival times.

Formal

$$\Pr[\mathcal{R}(\tau, j) \in S] \le e^\varepsilon \Pr[\mathcal{R}(\tau', j') \in S]$$ Sensitivity: $\Delta\eta_\Gamma = \max|\eta_\tau(j) - \eta_{\tau'}(j')|$.

At every job release, add random noise $Y$ to the inter-arrival time. The attacker sees a noisy version, not the true one.

Formal

$$\mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{time}}(\tau_i, j) = \eta_i(j) + Y$$

The biggest possible difference between any two admissible inter-arrival times. The “noise scale” we need to cover.

Formal

$$\Delta\eta_\Gamma = \max_{\tau,\tau' \in \Gamma,\, j,j' \in \mathbb{N}} |\eta_\tau(j) - \eta_{\tau'}(j')|$$

Standard DP, on inter-arrival times: any observed timing is within an $e^\varepsilon$ ratio across any two tasks and any two jobs.

Formal

$$\Pr[\mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{time}}(\tau, j) \in S] \le e^{\varepsilon_{\mathrm{time}}} \Pr[\mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{time}}(\tau', j') \in S]$$

At each scheduling moment, the state is: who's ready, what their deadlines are, and how much inversion budget each has left.

Formal

$$x(t) = \bigl(\mathcal{Q}(t),\, \{d_i\},\, \{v_i(t)\}\bigr)$$

How desirable is each ready task? Negative deadline distance — closer deadline, higher score, more likely to be picked.

Formal

$$u(x(t), \tau_i) = -(d_i - t), \quad \Delta u = \max_i d_i - \min_i d_i$$

Sample a task. Probability $\propto \exp(\varepsilon_{\mathrm{sel}} u_i / 2\Delta u)$, restricted to feasible tasks $\mathcal{F}(t)$.

Formal

$$\Pr[\mathcal{M}_{\mathrm{sel}}(x(t)) = \tau_i] = \frac{\mathbb{1}[\tau_i \in \mathcal{F}(t)]\,\exp\!\left(\frac{\varepsilon_{\mathrm{sel}} u_i}{2\Delta u}\right)}{\sum_{\tau_j \in \mathcal{F}(t)}\exp\!\left(\frac{\varepsilon_{\mathrm{sel}} u_j}{2\Delta u}\right)}$$

Same DP definition as Layer 1, but applied to the chosen task across two neighboring scheduler states.

Formal

$$\Pr[\mathcal{M}_{\mathrm{sel}}(x) = \tau] \le e^{\varepsilon_{\mathrm{sel}}}\Pr[\mathcal{M}_{\mathrm{sel}}(x') = \tau]$$

McSherry & Talwar 2007 — any exponential mechanism with score sensitivity $\Delta u$ is $\varepsilon$‑DP. Standard, not novel.

Formal

For any score $u$ with sensitivity $\Delta u$, the mechanism in Def. 3.6 is $\varepsilon_{\mathrm{sel}}$‑indistinguishable.

If Layer 1 is $\varepsilon_{\mathrm{time}}$‑DP and Layer 2 is $\varepsilon_{\mathrm{sel}}$‑DP, the joint observation is $(\varepsilon_{\mathrm{time}} + \varepsilon_{\mathrm{sel}})$‑DP.

Formal

$$\Pr[O \mid x] \le e^{\varepsilon_{\mathrm{time}} + \varepsilon_{\mathrm{sel}}}\Pr[O \mid x']$$ Dwork & Roth, Thm 3.16.

When Layer 1 is RDP-Gaussian, Rényi-DP composition is tighter than pure DP composition over many decisions.

Formal

$$D_\alpha(O\mid x \,\Vert\, O\mid x') \le \varepsilon_{\mathrm{time}}^{\mathrm{RDP}}(\alpha) + \varepsilon_{\mathrm{sel}}^{\mathrm{RDP}}(\alpha)$$

Over a hyperperiod with $H$ scheduling decisions and $J$ jobs per task, budgets add up. $H$ can be large — that's why we measure cumulative $\varepsilon$.

Formal

$$\varepsilon_{\mathrm{total}}^{\mathrm{pure}} = J \varepsilon_{\mathrm{time}} + H \varepsilon_{\mathrm{sel}}$$
